title: Production Monitoring
concept: Monitor generation latency, out-of-vocabulary rates, and user feedback loops to track model health in the wild. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Advanced Capstone.'

exercises.prompt: Why do we need to monitor AI models after they are launched?

options.text: To make the model train faster.
To track model health, speed, and accuracy in the real world.
To delete the model automatically if it makes a single mistake.

exercises.explain: Monitoring ensures that our AI remains fast, accurate, and relevant even when user behavior and language change over time.

cards.title: Is Your AI Healthy?
cards.body: Building an AI model is only half the battle. Once it's live, how do you know it's actually working well for your users? Without monitoring, your AI might start slowing down or outputting nonsense without you ever realizing it!

cards.title: What is Production Monitoring?
cards.body: Production Monitoring is the practice of tracking an AI model's performance in the real world. By checking how fast it responds (latency), the new words it sees (out-of-vocabulary rate), and what users think (feedback), we can fix issues before they ruin the user experience.

cards.title: The Restaurant Kitchen
cards.body: Think of your AI model like a restaurant kitchen. You can't just cook the food and walk away; you need to check how long it takes to serve a dish (latency), if ingredients are running out (out-of-vocabulary), and if customers are leaving bad reviews (user feedback). Monitoring keeps the kitchen running smoothly.

cards.title: Monitoring in Action
cards.body: Imagine a customer service chatbot. By monitoring it, you notice that its average response time jumped from 0.5 seconds to 5 seconds (high latency) because users started typing in a new slang term it didn't understand (out-of-vocabulary). You can now update the model quickly!

cards.title: Myth: Deploy and Forget
cards.body: Many believe that once a model is deployed, it will work perfectly forever. In reality, real-world data constantly changes, and models can slow down or degrade over time if they aren't carefully watched.

cards.title: Monitored vs. Blind
cards.body: Compare how a healthy monitored system behaves versus an unmonitored one when real-world conditions change.

left.text: Detects latency spikes and OOV words early, allowing developers to patch and improve the model instantly.

right.text: Slowly gets worse over time without anyone knowing, leading to frustrated users and broken apps.

exercises.prompt: What does the "out-of-vocabulary" (OOV) rate measure in production?

options.text: How much electricity the servers are consuming.
The rate of new, unrecognized words the model encounters in the wild.
The number of typos the AI makes in its own responses.
The speed at which the model generates text.

exercises.explain: Out-of-vocabulary (OOV) rates track how many new or unrecognized words the AI is encountering from real users compared to its training data.

exercises.prompt: True or False: User feedback loops are useless because computers can automatically detect all bad AI answers.
exercises.explain: User feedback (like ratings or flags) is crucial because automated metrics cannot always tell if a response was actually helpful or polite.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe response time.
exercises.sentence: When your AI takes too long to reply to a user, it has high [blank].
exercises.explain: Latency measures the time delay between a user's input and the AI's response. Keeping it low ensures a snappy user experience.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a direct user feedback loop for monitoring AI quality?

options.text: An AI model predicting the weather.
A user clicking a 'thumbs-down' button on a chatbot's response.
A database storing a list of user passwords.

exercises.explain: The thumbs-down button is a direct form of user feedback, letting developers know the AI's answer was not helpful.

exercises.prompt: When should you monitor an AI model's health in production?

options.text: Only after retraining a model from scratch.
Continuously in real time to catch slowdowns and bad predictions immediately.
Once a year during the company-wide holiday break.
Only when the model completely stops responding.

exercises.explain: Monitoring latency, vocabulary changes, and user feedback helps catch bugs early and ensures your AI remains fast and helpful in the real world. Next up: Advanced Capstone.
